coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical sensing platform for the detection of nitroaromatics using g-C3N4/V2O5 nanocomposites modified glassy carbon electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Sangamithirai, D.
Ramanathan, S.
coredata.dc:description: Rapid, reliable, cost-effective and trace-level detection of nitroaromatic toxins is becoming increasingly important because of their carcinogenic nature, and their presence in water sources as an environmental contaminant. The present study reports the development of an electrochemical sensing platform based on g-C3N4/V2O5 (VOCN) nanocomposites modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for the effective detection of nitroaromatics. The VOCN nanocomposites were synthesized via wet impregnation method, and were characterized by XRD, FT-IR, HRSEM and XPS techniques. The electrochemical behavior of four nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP) were investigated on seven nanomaterials-based sensors using cyclic voltammetry, square wave voltammetry and chronoamperometry techniques. Under optimized conditions, the nitroaromatic compounds exhibited reduction peaks corresponding to the number of nitro-functional groups on an aromatic ring. The electrochemical studies revealed that the composite with 7 wt% V2O5 (VOCN7/GCE) exhibits higher sensitivity for the detection of 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP. The VOCN7/GCE recorded the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.85 nM, 3.3 nM, 3.4 nM and 11.0 nM in a linear range between 1.0 nM - 100.0 µM, 10.0 nM - 100.0 µM, 10.0 nM - 100.0 µM and 30.0 nM - 100.0 µM for 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP, respectively. The fabricated sensor showed high reusability and reproducibility, and could detect the four nitroaromatics in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical sensing platform for the detection of nitroaromatics using g-C3N4/V2O5 nanocomposites modified glassy carbon electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Madras, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology, Madras, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid, reliable, cost-effective and trace-level detection of nitroaromatic toxins is becoming increasingly important because of their carcinogenic nature, and their presence in water sources as an environmental contaminant. The present study reports the development of an electrochemical sensing platform based on g-C3N4/V2O5 (VOCN) nanocomposites modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) for the effective detection of nitroaromatics. The VOCN nanocomposites were synthesized via wet impregnation method, and were characterized by XRD, FT-IR, HRSEM and XPS techniques. The electrochemical behavior of four nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP) were investigated on seven nanomaterials-based sensors using cyclic voltammetry, square wave voltammetry and chronoamperometry techniques. Under optimized conditions, the nitroaromatic compounds exhibited reduction peaks corresponding to the number of nitro-functional groups on an aromatic ring. The electrochemical studies revealed that the composite with 7 wt% V2O5 (VOCN7/GCE) exhibits higher sensitivity for the detection of 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP. The VOCN7/GCE recorded the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.85 nM, 3.3 nM, 3.4 nM and 11.0 nM in a linear range between 1.0 nM - 100.0 µM, 10.0 nM - 100.0 µM, 10.0 nM - 100.0 µM and 30.0 nM - 100.0 µM for 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP, respectively. The fabricated sensor showed high reusability and reproducibility, and could detect the four nitroaromatics in real water samples.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of aromatic compounds containing nitro-functional group(s) including nitrophenols and nitrotoluenes are of major significance for environmental safety measures, as they are part of volatile organic compounds [1–3]. The extensive utilization of these compounds in chemical and military industries contaminate the environment over the long-term [4]. Nitroaromatic compounds have been listed as “priority pollutants” by the United State environmental protection agency, due to their high toxic nature even at low concentrations, and are labelled as an environmental contaminants and human carcinogens [5]. The toxicity is understood to be due to the presence of easily reducible nitro group(s), which lead to the formation of nitro anion radical, and nitroso and hydroxyl‑amine derivatives [6]. These derivatives are the key in-charge of cytotoxic, carcinogenic and mutagenic behavior of the nitro-compounds and their toxic level increases with increasing number of nitro group on the aromatic ring.

4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) from the class of mononitrophenols, 2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP) under dinitrophenols, and 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP) from the group of trinitrophenols are the most widely used nitroaromatics in the production of dyes, pest control, wood preservatives, pharmaceuticals and explosives. Of all nitrophenols, TNP is particularly important because of the presence of three nitro groups, which leads to its higher toxicity compared to that of nitrophenols with one or two nitro-functional group(s) [7,8]. 2,4-Dinitrotoluene (DNT) is a nitroaromatic compound under nitrotoluenes group, which is also equally important as nitrophenols. DNT is used as a raw material for the synthesis of explosive 2,4,6-trinitroluene, and in munition industries as a modifier for smokeless powder [9]. The detection of these nitro compounds i.e., 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP in water bodies is important.

Nitroaromatics can be detected with various methods such as gas chromatography [10], liquid chromatography [11], capillary electrophoresis [12], fluorometric detection [13], surface enhanced-Raman scattering (SERS) [14], etc. These techniques require large and expensive instrumentation that typically need time-consuming sample preparation procedures, long analysis time and trained personnel. Electrochemical sensing techniques have been explored to detect nitroaromatics, with high sensitivity, selectivity and simple detection protocols [12,13]. Electrochemical methods exploit the reduction of nitro groups (which are the well-known electron acceptors) to generate signals, and every nitro group has its own finger print reduction peak.

Earlier studies on electrochemical sensing of nitrophenols and nitrotoluenes employ metallic and mixed-metallic nanoparticles [1,2,15–17], carbon-based metal/metal ion composite materials [18–21], molecular imprinted polymers [22], and aptamer-based materials [23]. However, many of these catalysts are of relatively higher cost, suffer from poor sensitivity and exhibit LOD in micromolar concentration. Electrochemical detection of these toxic compounds still remains as a challenging task, and requires development of suitable catalysts with high selectivity and sensitivity.

In recent times, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has drawn considerable attention in the field of catalysis [24–26]. g-C3N4 is synthesized from inexpensive precursors of C and N (urea, melamine), and is biocompatible and nontoxic. It exhibits a three-dimensional porous structure which can enhance the kinetics. However, if g-C3N4 particles agglomerate, then the effective surface area can reduce, leading to a reduction in its sensitivity.

To circumvent this issue, heterogeneous catalysts have been successfully developed with a strategy to anchor active sites on the surface of g-C3N4 to improve their catalytic performance. V2O5 nanoparticles are of interest, since they exhibit good electrocatalytic properties. It is reported that, V5+ ions play a dominant role in electrochemical redox reactions, and is responsible for the electrocatalytic activity of V2O5 [27–29]. However, the poor electronic conductivity of V2O5 and its high dissolution in liquid electrolyte, result in slow kinetics and poor long-term electrochemical performance [30]. The charge transfer properties of V2O5 can be improved by compositing it with carbonaceous materials, as reported by various research groups. Zhao et al. synthesized V2O5-rGO composites for application in supercapacitors and Li-ion batteries, and showed that the capacitive behavior and cycling performance were good [31]. Zhou et al. reported the preparation of porous g-C3N4@V2O5 composite electrode with improved electrochemical performance for supercapacitor applications [32]. Jayaraman et al. synthesized visible light active V2O5-g-C3N4 heterojunction and demonstrated that the composites are promising photocatalytic materials for efficient utilization of solar energy in environmental remedial applications [28]. Further, the g-C3N4-V2O5 composite for selective oxidation of olefins, using hydrogen peroxide as oxidant, has been reported by Zou et al. [29]. Thus, it is clear that the incorporation of V2O5 with carbon materials can effectively improve the charge transfer due to synergistic effect between the composite moieties.

To the best of our knowledge, there is no report on electrochemical detection of nitroaromatics using g-C3N4/V2O5 nanocomposite electrodes. In the present investigation, we introduce a binder free g-C3N4/V2O5 nanocomposite sensor to detect nitroaromatics. The amalgamation of V2O5 with g-C3N4 provides the benefit of a good conductivity with fast kinetics. As a result, the combination of g-C3N4 with V2O5 was found to be an effective, low cost, sensitive and selective electrochemical sensor, with distinct identification of each nitro group present in the four nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP).

Melamine (99% purity), Ammonium metavanadate (≥ 99% purity), 4-Nitrophenol (≥ 99% purity) 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (≥ 98%), were purchased from Merck. 2,4-Dinitrophenol (≥ 97% purity) and 2,4-Dinitrotoluene (≥ 97% purity) were purchased from SDFCL, India. Milli-Q water was used throughout the experiment. Acetic buffer solution (sodium acetate (≥ 99% purity) and acetic acid (≥ 99% purity, 0.1 M) and phosphate buffer solutions (sodium dihydrogen phosphate and disodium monohydrogen phosphate, 0.1 M) with various pH values were used. All the chemicals used for the preparation of buffer solutions were procured from Merck.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) was synthesized by thermal polycondensation of melamine. In a typical synthesis, 10 g of melamine was loaded in an alumina crucible (with a cover to prevent the sublimation of the precursors) and heated in air at 10 °C min−1 and was held at 550 °C for 4 h. After that, the furnace was cooled down to room temperature naturally. A yellow-coloured product was obtained, and it was washed in milli-Q water, centrifuged, dried and ground into fine powder for further use.

Vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) was prepared via hydrothermal method using ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3) as the starting material. 0.1 M NH4VO3 was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water. Dilute sulphuric acid (H2SO4/H2O = 1:4 v/v) was added dropwise to the above prepared solution until the pH was ⁓ 4. The mixture was stirred for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous, orange coloured solution. The solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and was kept at 120 °C for 24 h and then allowed to cool down to room temperature. The precipitate was collected, washed and centrifuged several times with milli-Q water and ethanol, and then dried at 70 °C for 8 h. The as-prepared sample was further heat treated at 550 °C for 1 h to get pure V2O5.

g-C3N4/V2O5 nanocomposite with various concentrations of V2O5 were synthesized using the following procedure: 0.5 g of as-prepared g-C3N4 with different concentrations of V2O5 (1, 3, 5, 7 and 10 wt%) were mixed with 40 mL of methanol. The mixture was subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min and then vigorously stirred at 80 °C until the methanol evaporated completely. The residue that remained was heated at 10 °C min−1 in a muffle furnace and was annealed at 300 °C for 2 h. The g-C3N4/ V2O5 nanocomposites with 1, 3, 5, 7 and 10 wt% of V2O5 are denoted as VOCN1, VOCN3, VOCN5, VOCN7 and VOCN10, respectively.

Glassy carbon rod (99.99%, Alfa Aesar) of 3 mm diameter and 5 mm length was used as the working electrode. The rod was embedded in a Teflon holder, exposing only one face to the electrolyte. Prior to the experiments, GCE was sequentially polished with 800, 1200 and 2000 grit emery paper, and then polished with 0.5 and 0.3 µm alumina powder in a polishing machine, to attain a smooth surface. The polished electrode was cleaned by ultra-sonication in acetone and milli-Q-water for 2 min and then dried in air at room temperature.

The synthesized g-C3N4/V2O5 nanocomposite was dispersed in ethanol (1 mg/mL) and ultra-sonicated for 30 min to attain a homogeneous suspension. Then, 2 µL of the prepared suspension was drop coated onto the surface of GCE and then dried at room temperature to obtain g-C3N4/V2O5 nanocomposite modified GCE (VOCN/GCE). Similarly, 2 µL of V2O5 in ethanol (1 mg/mL) and 2 µL of g-C3N4 in ethanol (1 mg/mL) was drop coated on GCE to get V2O5/GCE and g-C3N4/GCE, respectively.

The synthesized nanocomposites were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR), High-Resolution Scanning Electron Microscopy (HRSEM) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The XRD analysis was done by D8 Advance (Bruker) X-ray diffraction System with Cu Kα1 radiation with a wavelength of 1.5406 Å over the 2θ range from 10° to 55° at a scan rate of 2°/min. Infrared spectroscopy in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 was performed by using a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectroscope. The surface morphology was characterized using a S4800 SEM (Hitachi, Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 5 kV. XPS analysis was done by PHI 5000 Versa Probe III (Physical Electronics) equipped with Al anode as the X-ray source. Carbon peak (C1s) at 284.6 eV was used to correct the binding energy, and Shirley background subtraction was employed in the analysis.

Electrochemical experiments were performed through conventional three electrode cell arrangement using VERSASTAT4 electrochemical workstation. A Platinum wire was used as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl (in 3.5 M KCl) was used as the reference electrode. The working electrode was prepared as mentioned in Section 2.5. In this work, all the potentials are reported vs. Ag/AgCl. The impedance spectra were acquitted at open circuit potential, in a frequency range of 10 kHz to 1 Hz, and a potential amplitude of 10 mV was employed. The EIS data were validated using linear Kramers-Kronig Transform software [33]. Cyclic voltammograms were acquired by sweeping the potential at 20 to 200 mVs−1, and the exact sweep rate employed are presented in the respective figure captions. Square wave voltammetry was performed using a frequency of 50 Hz, a step height of 5 mV and a pulse amplitude of 25 mV. Chronoamperometry experiments were conducted by stepping the working electrode potential to the required value, and monitoring the current every 1 s.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the prepared V2O5, g-C3N4 and VOCN nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 1 . The XRD patterns recorded for V2O5 exhibited various diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 15.34°, 20.38°, 21.72°, 26.37°, 31.12°, 32.54°, 33.50°, 34.48°, 36.32°, 37.44°, 41.43°, 42.14°, 45.57°, 47.51°, 48.84°, 51.32°, and 52.28° These peaks are attributed to 200, 010, 110, 101, 400, 011, 111, 301, 211, 410, 020, 120, 411, 501, 600, 022 and 610 planes respectively, of the orthorhombic phase of V2O5 (ICDD card no. 98–002–1963) [34]. For g-C3N4, two distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ = 13.06° and 27.42° corresponding to (100) and (002) planes of hexagonal graphitic carbon nitride are observed [35]. In the case of VOCN nanocomposites, both diffraction peaks corresponding to hexagonal phase of g-C3N4 and the orthorhombic phase of V2O5 are visible, indicating the formation of the composite. Further, the peak intensity of V2O5 increases and the peak intensity of g-C3N4 decreases with increasing amount of V2O5 in the composite. The observed XRD patterns confirm the formation of g-C3N4/ V2O5 nanocomposites.

The FT-IR spectra of the synthesized V2O5, g-C3N4 and VOCN nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 2 . FT-IR spectrum of V2O5 exhibited three strong absorption bands at 608, 827 and 1007 cm−1. The bands at 608 and 827 cm−1 can be attributed to asymmetric stretching modes of V—O—V bond and the band at 1007 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of V = O bond [36]. For g-C3N4, the characteristic ring breathing mode of triazine units is observed at 804 cm−1. The peaks at 1007, 1225, 1300, 1390, 1533 and 1630 cm−1 are attributed to the presence of stretching vibrations of C = N and aromatic C—N heterocycles [37]. The strong bands recorded in the range of 3100–3300 cm−1 are associated with the stretching modes of N—H (—NH3 and =N—H) bonds of amines [38]. The FT-IR spectra of VOCN nanocomposites exhibited the overlap of V2O5 and g-C3N4 spectra, indicating the existence of both the moieties. The peak intensity of g-C3N4 is seen to decrease with an increase of V2O5 concentration in the composite. In the case of nanocomposites, the typical bands of g-C3N4 at 3100–3300 cm−1, 1200–1700 cm−1 and the triazine units at 804 cm−1 are clearly visible. This suggests that the structure of g-C3N4 is not altered by the incorporation of V2O5, and this is also confirmed by XRD results.

HRSEM images of g-C3N4, V2O5 and VOCN nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3 . g-C3N4 exhibited stacking layers of nanosheets with porous morphology, which is due to the strong covalent C—N bonding with SP2 hybridization (Fig. 3(a)). V2O5 showed a sequence of belt-like structure (Fig. 3(b)). After compositing with g-C3N4, the nanobelts of V2O5 are anchored onto the surface of g-C3N4 sheets as seen in Fig. 3(c-h). All the VOCN composites displayed the formation of matrix type nanocomposite, with a typical two-dimensional (2D) layered structure of g-C3N4 as a matrix phase over which the one-dimensional (1D) V2O5 nanobelts are anchored.

The V2O5 nanobelts are longer and highly-ordered in the composites compared to pure V2O5, which shows that V2O5 growth over g-C3N4 sheets is uniform. The numbers of V2O5 nanobelts increased with an increase in the V2O5 concentration in the composite material. The compact junction at the interface between g-C3N4 and V2O5 signifies the presence of effective surface contact, which ensures the transmission of electrons between the interfaces. In addition, energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis attached with HRSEM was performed to obtain the elemental mapping of the composite. As shown in Fig. 4 , the results of elemental mapping confirm the formation of VOCN composite with the desired compositions in the form of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), vanadium (V) and oxygen (O) without any impurity.

The surface composition and the chemical states of the prepared VOCN7 nanocomposite were determined using XPS analysis and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 5(a)) displayed the characteristic peaks of carbon, nitrogen, vanadium and oxygen. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 5(b)) showed two peaks at the binding energies of 288.0 and 284.6 eV. The peak observed at 284.6 eV corresponds to graphitic carbon atoms and the strong one appearing at 288.0 eV is assigned to SP2 hybridized carbon in the triazine ring (NC = N) coordination in g-C3N4 [39]. The core level spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 5(c)) energy level showed the SP2 hybridization of nitrogen atoms (CN = C) at the binding energy of 398.2 eV and the peak appearing at 400.0 eV is ascribed to CN-H bond [40]. Two peaks are seen at 524.3 and 517.0 eV for the deconvoluted spectrum of V 2p (Fig. 5(d)), and they are assigned to V5+2p1/2 and V5+2p3/2, respectively and confirms the V5+ state [41]. As for the spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 5(e)), the peak observed at 530.0 eV corresponds to O-V5+ functional groups of V2O5 [42]. The peaks at 532.0 and 533.5 eV represents hydroxyl (OH) and adsorbed water, respectively [42]. The XPS results confirm the presence of g-C3N4 and V2O5 in the prepared composite.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective and non-destructive method which enables analysis of the interfacial properties of the modified electrodes. Fig. 6 displays the Nyquist plots of the EIS of bare GCE and of GCE modified with g-C3N4, V2O5 and the VOCN nanocomposites in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4− as the redox probe.

The Nyquist plots of the EIS of all the electrodes displayed two features, a semicircle in the high-frequency region and a straight line in the low frequency region. The diameter of the high-frequency semi-circle in the Nyquist plot is the charge-transfer resistance (Rct), which is a measure of the charge-transfer kinetics of the redox probe at the electrode surface. As a direct and sensitive parameter, Rct value can be used to characterize the interfacial properties of surface-modified electrodes [43]. Bare GCE (Fig. 6) displayed a large semicircle diameter with a Rct value of 1403 Ω, indicating a large charge-transfer resistance and poor electrochemical kinetics. g-C3N4 and V2O5 exhibited Rct values of 885 Ω and 665 Ω respectively, which are still large values. In contrast, lower Rct values were observed for VOCN nanocomposites, suggesting facile electrochemical kinetics at the surface of nanocomposite modified GCE. The Rct values of VOCN modified electrodes decreased as the concentration of V2O5 increases in the composite material. These results indicate that the high loading of V2O5 in g-C3N4 can increase the conductivity of VOCN electrode materials. Compared to all other modified electrodes, VOCN7 and VOCN10 modified electrodes displayed the lowest Rct values of 145 Ω. It is worth noting that the Rct value of VOCN10 is the same as that of VOCN7, corroborating that 7 wt% of V2O5 is sufficient to enrich the conductivity of VOCN nanocomposite.

Cyclic voltammograms of VOCN1/GCE electrodes in samples containing the nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP) at several solution pH (3–10) values were recorded and the results are presented in Fig. S1. The reduction peak potentials of nitroaromatics decreased gradually as the pH of the electrolyte increased from 3.0 to 10.0, with the maximum peak current occurring at pH 6.0. Based on the CV results, a solution of pH 6.0 was chosen for subsequent experiments. Fig. 7 shows the direct comparison of the cyclic voltammograms (CV) recorded on blank solution, bare GCE, g-C3N4/GCE, V2O5/GCE and the optimized nanocomposite modified GCE (VOCN7/GCE), in 0.1 M of pH 6.0 buffer electrolyte containing 100 μM of the analytes (4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP). The optimization of VOCN composites modified GCE towards the detection of nitroaromatics are given in Fig. S2. In the presence of 4-NP (Fig. 7A), the CVs of modified electrodes showed a distinct irreversible reduction peak (R1), a pair of reversible oxidation (O1) and reduction (R2) peaks and a peak corresponding to the irreversible oxidation of phenolic group (O2). The reduction peak (R1) corresponds to the reduction of 4-NP to p‑hydroxyl-amino phenol. The redox peaks (O1 and R2) signify the oxidation of p‑hydroxyl-amino phenol to p-nitroso-phenol (O1) and the subsequent reversible reduction (R2), respectively [19].

CV curve of blank solution did not exhibit any peak in the chosen potential window as shown in Fig. 7A (curve (a)). In the presence of 4-NP, bare GCE displayed R1 at a potential of −0.85 V (Fig. 7A, curve (b)) with a peak current of −4.5 µA. The nanocomposite VOCN7/GCE showed R1 at −0.75 V (Fig. 7A, curve (e))) and the reversible redox peaks O1 and R2 at 0.3 and −0.15 V, respectively. The irreversible oxidation of the phenolic group (O2) takes place around 1.0 V. The results on VOCN7/GCE electrode exhibited a shift in reduction peak potential (R1) with a higher current value compared to those obtained on bare GCE. To study the effect of V2O5 and g-C3N4 on the electrochemical detection of 4-NP, the results on V2O5/GCE and g-C3N4/GCE electrodes in 100 µM 4-NP (pH 6.0) were compared. A comparison of the CV curve of Fig. 7A (curve (c)) with Fig. 7A (curve (d)) shows that the current is higher on g-C3N4/GCE electrode than on V2O5/GCE electrode. However, the current obtained for both these electrodes are lower than VOCN/GCE (Fig. 7A, (curve (e)). As presented in (Fig. S2(A)), the nanocomposites modified GCE exhibited identical potential values, however the peak current values depended on the concentration of V2O5 and g-C3N4. When the V2O5 concentration was increased from 1 to 7%, the R1 current values increased, and a further increase in the V2O5 concentration to 10% resulted in a decrease in the R1 current.

For the electrochemical detection of DNP (Fig. 7B), the cyclic voltammogram of the optimized nanocomposite modified GCE (VOCN7/GCE) showed two reduction peaks (R1 and R2) on the first cathodic scan at the potential values of −0.5 and −0.7 V, respectively. These two peaks are corresponding to the formation of hydroxylamine species from the step-wise reduction of two nitro groups of DNP. An oxidation process (O1) was observed on the first anodic scan at 0.2 V, and this peak is attributed to the oxidation of the formed hydroxylamine moieties to the corresponding nitrosamine. On the next cathodic scan, an additional reduction peak (R3) was recorded at 0.02 V, and this is attributed to the reduction of nitroso species (O1) to hydroxylamine. The oxidation (O2) of phenol group is observed at 0.6 V. Bare GCE displayed broad and non-distinguishable peaks with lower current values than other modified electrodes. g-C3N4/GCE, and V2O5/GCE exhibited well-resolved reduction peaks compared to GCE, however the current values are lowered than that of nanocomposite modified GCE. The cyclic voltammograms recorded on nanocomposites modified GCE and the optimization of the nanocomposite based on the current values of reduction peaks (R1 and R2) are shown in Fig. S2(B).

In the case of DNT (Fig. 7(C)), the CV studies of VOCN7/GCE showed two reduction peaks R1 and R2 (corresponding to two nitro groups of DNT) at −0.5 and −0.7 V, respectively. The reversible redox peaks O1 and R3 are observed at 0.3 and −0.03 V, respectively. Here again, the composite electrode with 7 wt% of V2O5 exhibited the maximum current response for the detection of DNT as seen from Fig. S2(C).

Three well-defined reduction peaks (R1, R2 and R3) at the potentials of −0.5, −0.75 and −0.95 V are observed in the cyclic voltammetric detection of TNP on VOCN/GCE as shown in Fig. 7(D). These three peaks appeared as a result of step-wise reduction of the three nitro groups of TNP. The reversible oxidation and reduction peak (O1 and R4) corresponding to the formation of nitrosamine and its reduction are seen at −0.17 and −0.15 V, respectively. The peak corresponding to O2 appeared at 0.34 V. Bare electrode (Fig. 7D, curve (b)) did not display recognizable peaks for TNP. V2O5/GCE and g-C3N4/GCE electrodes resulted in all the three peaks, and showed a higher current response compared to that of bare GCE. The composite with 7 wt% of V2O5 exhibited the highest current response for the detection of TNP (Fig. S2(D)).

From the CV studies, it is clear that the VOCN nanocomposite modified electrodes show a significant detection performance with enhanced peak current for all the four nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP) studied. The VOCN/GCE electrodes exhibited a better sensing performance than V2O5/GCE and g-C3N4/GCE electrodes due to the synergistic effect, which can accelerate the rate of electron transfer on the electrode surface and provides a good conduction pathway. The observed results demonstrate the capability of g-C3N4 as a viable support material for the dispersion of 1D V2O5 crystalline nanobelts. Based on these results, the following inferences can be made: (i) nitroaromatics could strongly attach to VOCN/GCE due to the good adsorption ability of the prepared nanocomposite, and (ii) the sensing efficiency of the VOCN nanocomposite modified electrode is greatly enhanced towards the detection of nitroaromatics due to the excellent electron transfer pathways, large surface-to-volume ratio and synergistic catalytic activity of g-C3N4 and V2O5.

Among the nanocomposite electrodes investigated, the highest electrocatalytic current was observed on VOCN7/GCE for the detection of 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP. HRSEM analysis shows that composite with 7 wt% V2O5 contains continuous and highly ordered V2O5 nanobelts over the surface of g-C3N4 and this can promote the charge transfer. On further increasing the V2O5 wt% in the composite (VOCN10), the reduction current values of nitroaromatics decreased notably, which is probably due to the complete coverage of g-C3N4 by V2O5 nanobelts. Based on the CV results, VOCN7/GCE was chosen for further electrochemical experiments to determine the concentrations of 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP using square-wave voltammetry.

Cyclic voltammetry experiments were carried out at several scan rates ranging from 20 to 200 mV/s, in the presence of 100 µM of 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP on VOCN7/GCE electrode, and the results are shown in Fig. S3. In all the cases, the peak current increases linearly with the scan rate, owing to the formation of diffusion layer closer to electrode. This results in more flux in the case of faster scan rates compared to that at slower scans. A plot of the peak current versus the square root of scan rate exhibits a linear relationship, as shown in the insets of Fig. S3 (IA, IIA, IIIA, and IVA). The logarithm plot of current as a function of scan rate is constructed for the studied nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT, and TNP) and the results are shown in Fig. S3(IB, IIB, IIIB, and IVB), respectively. The slope values obtained for all the nitroaromatics are found to be around 0.5, which indicates that the overall electrochemical reduction reaction of nitroaromatics is diffusion-controlled [44].

It is worth noting that, for each compound, with an addition of nitro group on the aromatic phenyl ring, a new reduction peak appears. A single reduction peak is obtained for 4-NP, two reduction peaks for DNP and DNT, and three reduction peaks for TNP, corresponding to the respective number of nitro groups. Based on this, the probable mechanism for the electrochemical sensing behavior of VOCN/GCE towards the nitroaromatics is presented in scheme 1.

The LOD was calculated as 0.85 nM for 4-NP, 3.3 nM for DNP, 3.4 nM for DNT and 11.0 nM for TNP. The slope of these calibration plots as given in the calibration equations, and the estimated LOD values, indicate the potential of the fabricated electrode (VOCN7/GCE) as a sensitive platform for the determination of nitroaromatic compounds. In order to confirm the best performance of the composite, SWV experiments were performed on bare GCE, g-C3N4/GCE, and V2O5/GCE for the detection of nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT, and TNP) and the results are shown in Fig. S4. Bare GCE resulted in poor sensing performance with a small and broad peak, and was not able to resolve the reduction peaks of the nitro-groups in the case of DNP, DNT, and TNP indicating the poor electrocatalytic behavior. Compared to bare GCE, the SWVs of g-C3N4/GCE, and V2O5/GCE exhibited well resolved reduction peaks corresponding to the step-wise reduction of nitro-groups of the nitroaromatics with higher current values, thereby suggesting the improved electrocatalytic activity of the modified electrode [18]. The modified electrodes not only enhanced the current values, but also resolved the reduction peaks of the nitro-groups. Higher currents values are observed in the case of nanocomposite modified electrode, which is due to the synergistic effect of g-C3N4/GCE, and V2O5/GCE. The calibration plots were constructed from the recorded SWV patterns and the corresponding slope values, linear ranges, and LOD values are tabulated in Table S1, which confirmed that the VOCN7/GCE exhibited better sensing performance than that of bare GCE, g-C3N4/GCE, and V2O5/GCE.

Table 1 shows the comparison of detection limits of the present study with the literature reports, which demonstrates the suitability of VOCN modified GCE for the trace level determination of nitroaromatics [17,19–21,47–60].

Selectivity and tolerance to the presence of other materials are important characteristics of any sensor. Therefore, the practical applicability of the VOCN/GCE sensor was analyzed using SWV and chronoamperometry (CA), to evaluate the selectivity and the competitive effect of interference species. Fig. 9
(A) shows the SWVs of VOCN7/GCE in presence of 50 µM TNP and 1 mM of other possible interfering species/metal ions such as phenol, toluene, chlorophenol, catechol, chlorobenzene, Ag+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Ni2+ and Co2+. The SWV response in the presence of interfering species/metal ions (1.0 mM) at a concentration higher than that of TNP (50 µM) showed negligible current response, with no change in the detection potential of TNP. Further, the addition of 50 µM TNP displayed higher current without change in potential values. The observed results suggest that the interference species does not alter the electrochemical sensing performance of the proposed VOCN7/GCE sensor and thus it is highly selective towards the detection of nitroaromatics.

Further, CA was performed to study the selectivity of nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP) and the effect of other interference species/metal ions. The experiments were conducted at three different potentials, viz., −0.7 V, −0.85 V and −0.35 V, corresponding to three reduction peaks of TNP as observed in SWV studies. The CA results obtained for all three potential values are shown in Fig. 9(B-D). For the analysis conducted at an applied potential of −0.35 V (Fig. 9(B)), the addition of 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP led to an increase in the current response. On the other hand, addition of interference species/metal ions (phenol, toluene, chlorophenol, catechol, chlorobenzene, Ag+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Ni2+ and Co2+) at concentration 10 times higher than that of nitroaromatics did not alter the current values significantly. At −0.7 V, the addition of DNP, DNT and TNP showed measurable current values whereas nil response was recorded for 4-NP and other interfering species (Fig. 9(C)).

On conducting the experiment at −0.85 V (which is the potential corresponding to the reduction of the third nitro group of TNP and the one that is not present in other three nitroaromatics i.e., 4-NP, DNP and DNT), the chrono plot showed a current output only with the addition of TNP (10.0 µM), and not with the addition of the other three nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT) with a concentration level higher (1.0 mM) than that of TNP (Fig. 9(D)). Nil interference was recorded for other inference species/metal ions also. Further addition of TNP to the mixture of nitroaromatics produced a clear current response, as expected. Thus, both SWV and CA techniques confirm the capability of VOCN/GCE towards highly selective sensing of nitroaromatics.

The reproducibility of the VOCN7/GCE was analyzed by carrying out SWV experiment, in the presence of 100 µM of nitroaromatics (4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP) for 10 times using the same electrode, and no measurable variation was detected in terms of reduction potential and current response. The results suggest that the electrochemical behavior of VOCN/GCE towards nitroaromatics is highly reproducible (Fig. S5). To study the repeatability of the proposed sensor, two VOCN7/GCE electrodes were prepared for each analyte (4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP) and their current output was measured using SWV analysis. The results showed a relative standard deviation of 1.4%, 1.3%, 1.7% and 2.0% for 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP, respectively, which indicate that the VOCN7/GCE sensor yield reproducible results (Fig. S6). The SWV technique was further utilized to evaluate the short-term and long-term storage stability of VOCN/GCE by comparing the reduction peak current of analytes (100 µM each) after storing the electrode for a period of 1 month and 3 months, with the initial SWV current response (Fig. S7). After 1 month, the VOCN7/GCE retained 95.2%, 93.4%, 93.2% and 90.5% of the initial current response for 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP, respectively, which implies that the electrodes are stable. The longer-term storage stability analysis was performed after three months of storage from the initial drop cast. The electrode retained 90.3%, 88.4%, 88.2% and 85.4% of its current response for 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP, respectively, which confirm the excellent stability of the proposed sensor.

To analyze the efficiency of the developed sensor, real sample analysis was performed on tap water using standard addition method. The square wave voltammetry (SWV) of real sample was conducted using tap water, which were spiked with different concentrations (0.2, 0.5 and 1.0 µM) of 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP. The prepared samples were determined using SWV technique and the calculated recovery amounts are summarized in Table 2 . The results show that the signal recovery varies between 90 and 103%, affirming the suitability of the sensor for real sample analysis. Further, to evaluate the performance of the sensor to detect the nitroaromatic species in the nanomolar region, SWV experiments were performed by spiking 4-NP with nanomolar amount (1 nM to 10 nM) in natural water samples, and the obtained results are given in Table S2. The signal recoveries are observed in the range from 99.4 to 102.0%, indicating the efficiency of the proposed sensor to detect the target species in the nanomolar region.

In summary, we report the facile synthesis of g-C3N4-V2O5 (VOCN) nanocomposites via a simple and cost-effective wet-impregnation method using g-C3N4 and V2O5. A series of VOCN nanocomposites were synthesized with different wt% of V2O5. The formation of phase, purity and interaction between V2O5 and g-C3N4 in the composites were confirmed by several characterization techniques such as XRD, FTIR, HR-SEM, EDS elemental mapping, and XPS. The prepared VOCN nanocomposites were drop casted on GCE and fabricated as modified electrode sensor for the electrochemical determination of four nitroaromatics i.e., 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP. The modified electrode showed a step-wise reduction of the nitro groups of 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP. Due to the synergistic effect of V2O5 and g-C3N4 in the nanocomposite, there was an enhancement in the electrocatalytic properties of composite, resulting in a much better electrochemical sensing performance than bare GCE and pure g-C3N4 and V2O5 modified GCE. Interestingly, it could detect the nitroaromatics at trace-level with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.85 nM, 3.3 nM, 3.4 nM and 11.0 nM for 4-NP, DNP, DNT and TNP, respectively. The sensor showed high sensitivity and selectivity for nitroaromatics in the presence of other interfering species/metal ions and could also effectually distinguish between different nitroaromatics. The VOCN/GCE exhibited good reproducibility and storage stability and also revealed satisfactory results with tap water samples. Thus, a simple and cost-effective wet-impregnation method for the preparation of g-C3N4/V2O5 nanocomposite towards electrochemical detection of various nitroaromatics is reported. This sensor is very promising for the trace-level detection and distinction of nitroaromatics with high sensitivity, and selectivity in complex environmental samples.
